data.title: American Express Outlook Remains Positive Despite Recent Volatility
data.description:
American Express (AXP) still looks like a pretty solid investment to me and the past few quarters have only reinforced that view. Since my last Buy call, the stock's basically gone nowhere. It did climb to a new high around $387, but then gave those gains back after President Donald Trump floated the idea of a one-year cap on credit card interest rates at 10% and the Credit Card Competition Act. Even so, the stock is still up roughly 10% over the past year overall. I did mention before that the price was a bit steep, but the company's results and higher earnings show that paying up made sense here.

Premium Customers Continue to Drive Growth

A big reason for this is the kind of customers Amex has. The card base is mostly affluent and pretty steady, over 151 million cards now and a lot of the new accounts are for their premium cards. Amex has been putting money into making its cards feel more high-end and it's working. Card fee income has grown 17% a year since 2019. Just in Q4, card fee revenues were up 17% from the year before. Management expects card fees to keep growing in the mid- to high-teens as the new Platinum pricing settles in. They're really pushing Platinum and other premium cards. You can see it not just in the revenue numbers, but in how much more Platinum cardholders spend. According to management, Platinum accounts spend about ten times what a basic Blue Cash card does. This isn't just a vanity stat, it means higher average revenue per account and it costs less for every dollar spent.Operating leverage has improved as premium products scale, helping offset higher marketing and product investments.

Spending Growth Shows Strong Customer Engagement

The numbers from their network back this up. In Q4, total volume was up 9%. If you break it down, international card services went up 12% and U.S. consumer services were up 9%. Growth among younger customers remains strong, with Gen-Z spending growth particularly robust in reported segments. That's important because it shows Amex isn't just swapping out old customers for the same kind of people. They're actually bringing in younger, digital-first spenders. That should help keep card fees and network volumes growing and it answers the worry that Amex's customers are getting too old.

Credit Quality and Returns Remain Strong

Credit performance keeps holding up AXP's better-than-average returns. Net write-offs sit at 2.1%. Delinquency ratios are at 1.3%, which is lower than what most of the industry sees. That shows AXP is still picking customers who usually pay their bills and keeps hanging on to people with good credit. Return on equity came in at about 34%. The CET1 ratio sticks within management's 10-11% target range, so they've got room to keep buying back stock and bump the dividend 16% to $0.95 a share.

Earnings Growth and Guidance Support the Outlook

Management bumped up their medium-term revenue growth outlook to 9-10% for 2026. They're calling for EPS between $17.30 and $17.90. Consensus lands at $17.55, which falls right in line with the trend. For all of 2025, AXP pulled in $72.2 billion in net revenue (that's up 10% over last year). Net income moved up 7% to $10.8 billion. Adjusted EPS grew 15% to $15.38. Looking to 2027, consensus has EPS getting to $20.09 on $86 billion in revenue. Even if sales growth cools down, strong margins and stable credit risk should still bring steady double-digit profit growth.

Right now, the stock trades at about 20 times forward earnings. That's not cheap but that price makes sense: AXP keeps putting up returns on equity in the mid-30s and keeps beating on earnings. This price already assumes the company will keep growing at a double-digits pace. Still, the latest guidance and growth among younger customers and in international markets mean there's a chance results could beat what analysts expect, especially if younger and global users keep coming in at the rates we've seen. If management keeps hitting their marks, EPS might go above $20 and help both with buybacks and pushing the share price higher.

Risks Exist but the Growth Story Remains Intact

What really makes AXP stand out is that there aren't many true substitutes for what they do: their model mixes spending-based revenue, premium card fees and the ability to pull value from both their own products and partner cards. The engine for card fee growth still has a clear path as they roll out the new Platinum card. Management keeps pushing for better digital customer experiences too, including new AI projects and more international merchants. These could add extra revenue sooner than most people expect.

Regulators are pushing harder lately. Stuff like political talk around rate caps and the Credit Card Competition Act, those are getting louder and they could hit the business in a real way. Sudden drops in how confident people feel or what's happening with jobs could hurt, too. If rules come in that cut into card fees or squeeze interchange, it could really push down earnings power if the government moves fast. If revenue growth drops below 8%, credit losses drift up and fees start getting squeezed, Wall Street would likely have to cut its EPS forecasts to under $16. That could push the stock's high-end valuation down by 15-20%. For now though, credit quality actually looks better, December numbers show that net charge-offs and late payments are ticking down.

Investors are optimistic, but not getting carried away. The stock jumped after Q3's strong results, but didn't really move much when Q4 earnings missed (mostly because of higher spending and not as many new cards signed up). That shows investors are aware there's risk in running the business well, even if the company keeps beating the competition. If you're going to argue against being bullish, you'd need to see credit trends really get worse, people stop signing up for premium cards, or regulators move fast and actually pass tough new rules. Right now, none of that's happening. As long as management keeps doing what they're doing, risk and reward are still tilted in favor of owning the stock. The upside isn't just about the stock price going up, it's about Amex being able to keep growing earnings by over 10% and grabbing more revenue from both fees and the network. If revenue or earnings start missing, or if new rules show up and start to clearly drag down profits, then it's time to rethink. But for now, those problems aren't showing up.

I'm sticking with a Buy on American Express. They lead the premium card space, keep putting up strong numbers, the stock's not overpriced and there's a good chance for more upside. The company keeps finding ways to make more money from both old and new customers and they're controlling costs and keeping credit losses low. If Amex can't keep growing card fees, expand globally, or keep credit quality high, or if new regulations actually start hurting profits, I'll change my view. For now, though, the risk-reward setup still looks pretty favorable.

data.title: Hard Assets Weekly: Silver Price Could Multiply By 2.6x
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate my buy recommendation on assets that track the main hard assets. This article is part of a weekly series that started on January 6, 2025, where I bring valuable insights into investments in the most diverse commodities. This week, the topics covered will be insights into gold, silver, and oil, as well as the risks to the success of these investment theses.

Context

Anyone who reads my weekly articles knows about my constructive view for the gold and silver thesis, even after the collapse seen a few weeks ago. It is very curious to note that the flow of investments into gold has accelerated strongly when we analyze a window since 2020.

Despite the recent collapse, cumulative flows have reached $127 billion since 2020. This occurs even in a scenario where the price of gold is historically high in nominal terms.

The fact is that there are solid arguments for this demand dynamic for gold to continue in the coming years. One of these factors comes from supply, and in this sense, the graph below shows that there have been no relevant gold discoveries for two years, and this has never happened before. Therefore, gold is becoming increasingly scarce.

In parallel, many investors have difficulty seeing the relevance that China's gold purchases have in the general context of the gold thesis. Therefore, the graph below is very revealing and shows gold stocks in China from 2009 to 2025, and we see how they have risen impressively since the end of 2024. You can find my thesis on gold here.

Meanwhile, silver is a precious metal that has a correlation with gold. In my research to write this article, I came across a very interesting insight. When we adjust for inflation for silver, we see that despite the slide, if the metal reaches historic levels, it could still rise 2.6x to reach 1980s levels. You can find my thesis on silver here.

Finally, it is also interesting to analyze the oil. While prices are extremely pressured by expectations that the United States will carry out a major attack on the Iranian regime, in parallel we have a situation where the first decline in oil production in the United States has occurred in the last five years. All of this puts pressure on oil prices and makes it a very attractive investment, at least in my opinion. I was able to describe this thesis in another article.

All the insights listed above corroborate my optimistic view for investments in hard assets such as gold, silver, and oil. But, of course, we need to analyze the risks too.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

Investments in hard assets present intrinsic risks related to geopolitics that are difficult to measure. Additionally, in each thesis, I explain the particular risks of each asset; for example, below we can see that hedge funds have been reducing their exposure to gold in recent weeks, mainly due to the collapse in prices.

However, the main risk may be the Trump administration's attempt to change some fundamentals of the American economy, and I explain this in detail.

Recently, the American Treasury Secretary, Scott Bessent, said that a strong dollar is a priority for the US. Therefore, you should not worry about the recent weakness seen in the dollar. The main objective of US tariff policy is to incentivize companies to make factories domestically, allowing them to avoid export duties.

In this sense, the United States should, over time, see a reduction in tariff revenue and an increase in tax revenue. Because, in theory, companies should build their factories in the United States, and there will be a greater demand for dollars. Therefore, the recent fall in the dollar is much more explained by the heating of previously stagnant economies.

This is interesting as it comes amid 15-year price support that the dollar is reaching.

It is important to highlight that if the Trump administration manages to implement these fundamentals, the American economy will reindustrialize and strengthen, especially the fiscal situation, and the strong dollar together with this fiscal improvement should reduce the demand for hard assets, especially gold. Therefore, investors must pay attention to the combination of tariff policy and fiscal health in the United States.

The Bottom Line

In this article I was able to share more relevant insights about investment theses in hard assets such as gold, silver, and oil. It is important to note that the recent rout in precious metals prices has not changed my constructive outlook for these assets.

In parallel, oil, which until then was frowned upon by many investors who believed in its price falling, has stood out and risen. Geopolitical fears and tight balances between supply and demand drive the price of this strategic commodity.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate the recommendation to buy assets that track the main hard assets. In my opinion, the entire economic context in which we are located will continue to boost these assets, but this does not prevent investors from monitoring the American fiscal situation and its tariff policy, which can have a major impact on the pricing of these assets.

data.title: American Century High-Yield Municipal Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Munis logged a quarterly gain. Municipal bonds (munis) delivered their second straight quarterly gain, improving year-to-date performance. A favorable technical backdrop, combined with continued inflows and Federal Reserve easing, helped support the asset class.

Economy remained resilient. The government shutdown canceled or delayed key economic data, but available metrics indicated resilience. The U.S. economy gathered strength in the third quarter, logging an annualized growth rate of 4.3%, compared with 3.8% in the second quarter. Other data released in the fourth quarter showed economic activity slowed but generally remained expansionary.

Munis outperformed Treasuries. Longer-maturity municipal bond yields declined for the quarter, which helped investment-grade munis outperform Treasuries. Additionally, the yield curve flattened, general obligation ('GO') bonds outperformed revenue bonds and longer-maturity munis broadly outpaced shorter-maturity issues.

Most states' finances were sound. Overall, solid financial market performance has kept most states' reserve balances on solid ground. Many states expect to draw down surplus funds as pandemic-era federal support wanes and budget challenges emerge.

Additionally, federal budget cuts will lead to higher Medicaid costs, but the extended timeline for implementation may offset funding gaps.

Issuance remained robust. Fourth-quarter municipal bond issuance totaled $141.6 billion, down from $155 billion in the third quarter but slightly higher compared with 2024's final quarter. Issuance in the latest quarter was particularly heavy in the electric power and energy prepay sectors. Total muni issuance in 2025 topped $580 billion, a 13% increase compared with 2024, according to SIFMA.

High-yield munis underperformed. Municipal credit spreads remained fairly steady for the quarter. High-yield municipal bond returns advanced but lagged investment-grade munis for the period. Meanwhile, ratings upgrades to downgrades were nearly even, and defaults broadly declined.

Portfolio Performance Review

Yield curve effects drove results. Our yield curve strategy broadly contributed to fourth-quarter performance compared with the benchmark. Duration positioning, avoiding intermediate-duration bonds and price increases as bonds approach maturity all aided relative performance.

Security selection aided performance. Security selection lifted results compared with the benchmark, largely due to our choices in the industrial development revenue/pollution control revenue sector. These choices more than offset negative selection effects in the retirement community and tobacco sectors.

Sector allocations hindered results. Sector positioning was a modest detractor compared with the benchmark, mainly due to weightings in the tobacco, energy prepay and local GO sectors. However, positioning in the retirement community sector provided a positive offset.

Positioning for the Future

Stimulus may aid economy. We expect a K-shaped economy to persist near term, as higher-income Americans drive spending and middle- and lower-income earners face rising unemployment and wage pressures. However, fiscal stimulus, including nearly $150 billion in consumer tax benefits, a pro-growth regulatory backdrop and business spending on artificial intelligence may aid the economy.

Supply/demand dynamic remains solid. In our view, municipal bond issuance should remain robust, largely due to infrastructure needs, higher building costs and more refunding. Demand for municipal bonds has been strong, and we expect that trend to continue as investors seek potential tax-efficient income, liquidity and tactical flexibility.

Select sectors look attractive. Solid reserves and liquidity should fuel stable backdrops for traditional tax-backed and essential service sectors. Some nontraditional sectors (energy prepay, charter schools, student housing) also appear stable. As fiscal policies unfold, health care and state sectors may face pressures, while regulatory easing may help public power and utilities bonds.

Commentary Glossary

Adjustable-Rate Mortgages (ARMs): Mortgages in which the interest rate is adjusted periodically according to a specific index.

Agency Securities (Agencies): Debt securities issued by U.S. government agencies.

Agency MBS: Mortgage-backed securities issued by U.S. government agencies.

Alternative Minimum Tax ('AMT'): A parallel tax system that was created to keep high income individuals from avoiding taxes through various deductions and exemptions.

Asset-Backed Securities ('ABS'): A form of securitized debt backed by loan assets such as auto loans, student loans, and credit card debt.

Basis Points ('BPS'): Used to express percentage values that are carried out to two decimal places. One basis point equals 0.01%.

Breakeven Rate: Yield difference between nominal Treasury notes and TIPS; indicates the market's expectations for inflation.

Carry-Oriented Currencies: Higher-yielding currencies of countries where interest rates are generally higher than those of countries with lower-yielding currencies. These higher-yielding currencies are targeted for "carry trades," where investors borrow money in a low-interest rate currency and invest in a higher yielding currency, potentially profiting from the difference in interest rates.

Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLOs): A form of securitized debt, typically backed by pools of corporate loans and their payments.

Collateralized Mortgage Obligations (CMOs): A form of structured, securitized debt derived from mortgage-backed securities, typically backed by pools of residential mortgages and their payments.

Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities ('CMBS'): Securities representing ownership in pools of commercial real estate loans used to finance the construction and improvement of income-producing properties such as hotels, shopping centers, and office buildings.

Commercial Paper: Short-term debt issued by corporations to raise cash and to cover current expenses in anticipation of future revenues.

Commodity: Basic raw materials such as precious metals and natural resources.

Consumer Price Index ('CPI'): Published by the U.S. government, CPI is the most commonly used statistic to measure inflation in the U.S. economy. Core CPI excludes food and energy prices, which tend to be volatile.

Contribution to Duration ('CTD'): A measure of bond portfolio risk that reflects a bond sector's contribution to the overall duration of the portfolio.

Corporate Debt: Debt instruments issued by private corporations.

Covered Bonds: Debt securities backed by cash flows from pools of mortgages or public sector loans. The asset pools help secure or "cover" these bonds if the originating financial institution becomes insolvent.

Coupon Interest Rate: The interest rate that is assigned to an interest-paying fixed income security when it is issued.

Credit Analysis: An analysis of the financial strength of the issuer of a security, and the ability of that issuer to provide timely payment of interest and principal.

Credit Default Swap: Credit derivative contracts between two counterparties that can be used to hedge credit risk or speculate on the changes in the credit quality of a corporation or government entity.

Credit Quality: Measures (usually in terms of high or low) the ability of issuers of debt securities to make timely interest and principal payments.

Credit Risk: The risk that the inability of the issuers of debt securities to make payments will cause these securities to decline in value.

Currency Overlay: A financial trading strategy used to separate the management of currency risk from other portfolio strategies. A currency overlay manager can seek to hedge the risk from adverse movements in exchange rates, and/or attempt to profit from tactical currency views.

Debt Service: The amount of money required within a given period to keep current on required/scheduled repayments of outstanding debt, including interest and principal.

Deflation: A decline in prices for goods, assets and services.

Derivatives: Securities whose performance and/or structure is derived from the performance and/or structure of other assets, interest rates or indexes.

Duration: A measure of the price sensitivity of a fixed income investment to changes in interest rates. The longer the duration, expressed in years, the more a fixed income investment's price will change when interest rates change.

Emerging Markets ('EM') Debt: Debt issued by countries whose economies are considered to be developing or emerging from underdevelopment.

Excess Return: The return of an investment portfolio minus the return of what is considered to be a relatively risk-free asset, such as a U.S. Treasury bill.

Exchange-Traded Fund ('ETF'): Represents a group of securities but is traded on an exchange like an individual stock.

Federal Funds Rate: An overnight interest rate banks charge each other for loans.

Federal Open Market Committee ('FOMC'): The committee that sets interest rate and credit policies for the Federal Reserve System, the U.S. central bank.

Federal Reserve (Fed): The U.S. central bank, responsible for monetary policies affecting the U.S. financial system and the economy.

Fundamental Factors: Economic or financial data used in determining asset value.

Futures Contracts (Futures): Agreements to buy or sell a specific amount of a commodity or financial instrument at a particular price on a stipulated future date.

General Obligation ('GO') Bonds: Municipal bonds that are secured by the full faith and credit of the issuer, including its taxing power.

Gross Domestic Product ('GDP'): A measure of the total economic output in goods and services for an economy.

Government-Sponsored Enterprises (GSEs): Privately owned corporations created by Congress to provide funding and help to reduce the cost of capital for certain borrowing sectors of the economy such as homeowners, students and farmers.

Headline Risk: Refers to the risk that a negative news media headline about one security issuer, incident or sector could affect the demand for and pricing of a much wider swath of securities.

Hedge: An investment designed to reduce the risk of an adverse price move in another investment. Often a hedge consists of taking an offsetting position in a related investment, such as a futures contract.

High-Yield ('HY') Debt: Fixed income securities with lower credit quality and lower credit ratings. High-yield securities are rated below BBB-.

Hybrid Adjustable-Rate Mortgages (ARMs): Combine the characteristics of fixed-rate and adjustable-rate mortgages, with rates remaining fixed for a set period of time and then adjusting periodically according to a specific index.

Inflation: An economic condition of rising prices for goods, services and assets, or equivalently, a declining value of money. Core inflation excludes food and energy prices, which tend to be volatile. It is the opposite of Deflation.

Inflation-Indexed Securities: Debt securities that offer returns adjusted for inflation. Typically, the principal of inflation-indexed securities is indexed to a widely used inflation measure and adjusted accordingly. Interest payments can be adjusted as well.

Interest Rate Risk: The risk that a fixed income investment's value will change due to changes in interest rates.

Investment-Grade ('IG') Debt: Fixed income securities with relatively high credit quality and credit ratings in the upper ranges of those provided by credit rating services. Using Standard & Poor's ratings as the benchmark, investment-grade securities are those rated BBB- or higher.

Leverage: The use of financial instruments and/or borrowed capital to increase potential returns or to increase purchasing power.

Long Position: Typical ownership of an asset or investment that gives the owner the right to transfer ownership, the right to any income generated by the asset, and the right to any profits or losses due to value changes.

Long/Short Position: A "market neutral" investing strategy that involves taking long positions in assets that are expected to increase in value and short positions in assets that are expected to decrease in value.

Master Limited Partnerships (MLPs): Publicly traded, generally higher-yielding securities of enterprises that engage in certain businesses, usually pertaining to the use of natural resources.

Mortgage-Backed Securities ('MBS'): A form of securitized debt that represents ownership in pools of mortgage loans and their payments.

MSCI ACWI (All Country World Index): A free float-adjusted market capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure the equity market performance of developed and emerging markets.

Municipal Securities: Debt securities typically issued by or on behalf of U.S. state and local governments, their agencies or authorities to raise money for a variety of public purposes, including financing for state and local governments as well as financing for specific projects and public facilities.

Nominal Yield: The interest rate to par value that the bond issuer promises to pay bond purchasers.

Non-Agency Mortgage-Backed Securities ('MBS'): MBS are groups of home mortgages that are sold by the issuing banks and then packaged together into "pools" and sold as a single security. Non-agency MBS are issued by private entities, such as financial institutions.

Personal Consumption Expenditures ('PCE'): This price deflator which comes from the Bureau of Economic Analysis' quarterly report on U.S. gross domestic product is based on a survey of businesses and is intended to capture the price changes in all final goods, no matter the purchaser. Because of its broader scope and certain differences in the methodology used to calculate the PCE price index, the Federal Reserve holds the PCE deflator as its preferred, consistent measure of inflation over time.

Quantitative Easing ('QE'): A form of monetary policy used by central banks to stimulate economic growth by purchasing domestic government securities to increase the domestic money supply, lower interest rates and encourage investors to make investments in riskier assets such as stocks and high-yield securities.

Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs): Securities that trade like stocks and invest in real estate through properties or mortgages.

Real Yield: A yield that has been adjusted to remove the effects of inflation.

Revenue Bonds: Municipal bonds that are secured by the net revenues from the project or facility being financed.

S&P 500 Index: The S&P 500® Index is composed of 500 selected common stocks most of which are listed on the New York Stock Exchange. It is not an investment product available for purchase.

Secondary Market: A market where investors make purchases from other investors, rather than from the initial issuers.

Securitized Debt: Debt resulting from aggregating debt instruments into a pool of similar debts, then issuing new securities backed by the pool. ABS, MBS, and CMOs are common forms of securitized debt.

Short Position: Refers to the sale of an asset borrowed, not owned, by the seller in anticipation of a price decline. If the seller can buy the asset later (to return what was borrowed) at a lower price, a profit results. If the price rises, the borrower/seller suffers a loss.

Sovereign Debt: A country's government-issued debt, priced in its native currency, which can be sold to investors in other countries.

Spreads, Maturity Spreads, Credit Spreads: Measured differences between two interest rates or yields that are being compared with each other. Spreads typically are measured between fixed income securities of the same credit quality, but different maturities (maturity spreads), or of the same maturity, but different credit quality (credit spreads).

Spread Sectors: Non-Treasury debt sectors with securities that usually trade at higher yields than comparable-maturity U.S. Treasury securities. These sectors typically trade at higher yields than Treasuries because they usually have relatively higher credit risk and/or more risk of prepayment.

Spread Widening, Tightening: Changes in spreads that reflect changes in relative value, with spread widening usually indicating relative price depreciation and spread tightening indicating relative price appreciation.

Swaps: Two-party contracts entered into primarily by institutional investors for periods ranging from a few weeks to more than one year. In a standard "swap" transaction, two parties agree to exchange the returns (or differentials in rates of return) earned or realized on particular predetermined investments or instruments.

Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities ('TIPS'): Inflation-linked debt securities issued by the U.S. Treasury.

Technical Factors: Market price behavior, trends, volume, and momentum data used in determining asset value.

U.S. Treasury securities: Debt securities issued by the U.S. Treasury and backed by the direct "full faith and credit" pledge of the U.S. government. Treasury securities include bills (maturing in one year or less), notes (maturing in two to 10 years) and bonds (maturing in more than 10 years). They are generally considered among the highest quality and most liquid securities in the world.

Variable-Rate Demand Notes (VRDNs): Short-term debt securities that track market interest rates using periodic (daily, weekly, monthly, or quarterly) interest rate adjustments.

Weighted Average Life to Maturity: The average time in years to receive the principal repayments.

Yield: A rate of return for bonds and other fixed-income securities. There are several types of yields and yield calculations.

Yield Curve: A line graph showing the yields of fixed income securities from a single sector from a range of different maturities at a single point in time.

Yield to Maturity: A common performance calculation for fixed income securities, which takes into account total annual interest payments, the purchase price, the redemption value, and the amount of time remaining until maturity.

Real Yield to Maturity: Yield to maturity minus any "inflation premium" that had been added/priced in.

Zero-Coupon Securities: Debt securities that are sold at a deep discount then redeemed for their full face value at maturity.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Baron Focused Growth Fund Q4 2025 Performance Insights: Portfolio Drivers & Decisions
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Baron Focused Growth Fund (BFGFX) Q4 2025 Shareholder Letter.

Aside from two of our private investments, SpaceX and xAI successfully completing capital raises at valuations significantly higher than previous marks, performance in the fourth quarter was led by our exposure to consumer-focused investments as the consumer remains resilient despite macro worries.

These gains were partially offset by continued concerns about the introduction of AI into the economy and those businesses that could be impacted most from the new competition. These included our subscription-based software and platform investments such as Spotify Technology S.A. (SPOT), CoStar Group, Inc. (CSGP), and Guidewire Software, Inc. (GWRE) However, while the increased competition hurt the valuation of these stocks in the quarter, it has not impacted financials, and these companies continue to generate strong revenue growth and margins in line with company and investor expectations.

Shares of FIGS increased 69.8% in the fourth quarter and added 161 bps to performance. The company continues to generate strong results, and revenue has recently accelerated from investments made over the past year. This is despite making a conscious decision to pull back on promotions. The company is now seeing more normal purchasing and replenishment trends while also benefitting from specific initiatives around product and marketing that are resonating with its customers. FIGS is also seeing better efficiencies out of its new fulfillment center that are resulting in strong margin improvement as well. We continue to believe FIGS can take share in the attractive $80 billion global health care apparel industry. Health care remains one of the fastest growing employment industries in the U.S. and FIGS benefits from being a need-based replenishment item. The company is well positioned to take share in the industry through its premium, high-quality products. In addition, we believe FIGS will also see growth from its initiatives to grow stores, business to business revenue, and expand internationally. We believe the revenue acceleration combined with margin expansion and strong cash generation should leave the company well positioned for further growth over time.

Shares of Hyatt Hotels (H) increased 13.1% and added 60 bps to performance in the fourth quarter as the company reported strong RevPAR and unit growth rates despite concerns about a deterioration in the macro economy. In addition, the company also came to an agreement with Chase (JPM) to extend its credit card agreement with stronger economics for Hyatt given the increase in the company's World of Hyatt membership. Finally, the company continues to sell its owned hotels at accretive rates and is using the proceeds to buy back the company's stock. Hyatt continues to have an investment grade balance sheet with 90% of the business coming through fees, yet trades at a discount to peers despite similar growth and mix of business. We believe this discount should narrow over time as investors see the continued growth and resilience of its business model.

Premium footwear and apparel brand On Holding (ONON) increased 9.8% and added 55 bps to performance in the fourth quarter. The company continues to generate strong revenue growth with accelerated margin expansion despite concerns about tariffs and increased competition from Nike (NKE). On's strong brand and premium positioning is allowing it to offset tariff exposure through selective price increases, while demand for its products remains resilient. The company should continue to grow for many years to come while taking share in the highly attractive global sportswear market. They remain a small player in a large growing market with just 2% of the global sports footwear market. We believe On has differentiated itself through its engineered solution and that the company's innovation capabilities should fuel share gains for many years. This growth should be supported by expansions across categories, retail outlets, and geographies. We believe the company should be able to grow revenue at a CAGR of over 20% the next few years leading to EBITDA growth of over 30%, which when combined with a mid-single-digit yield on free cash flow should set the stock up for strong returns in the years to come.

Spotify declined by 16.8% in the fourth quarter and detracted 130 bps from performance as investors were concerned with the decision by CEO Dan Ek to step down as CEO. In addition, further concerns about the timing of price increases and resulting margin expansion also frustrated investors. However, the company continues to institute price increases across multiple regions and complete negotiations with major record labels. User growth remains strong growing at a double-digit rate with high engagement and low churn even with price increases. The company remains on a path to increase gross margins through its high-margin artist promotions marketplace, growing podcast contribution, and ongoing investments in advertising where revenue growth is expected to accelerate this year. We continue to view Spotify as a long-term winner in music streaming with potential to reach 1 billion-plus subscribers by 2030.

CoStar declined by 20.3% in the fourth quarter and detracted 93 bps from performance as investors were concerned with a deceleration of growth in its core commercial real estate business and a slower-than-expected ramp of its residential business. However, we believe daily active users on its Homes.com platform should accelerate this year as its marketing investments begin to generate returns. Monthly active users have already reached 110 million and compare to Zillow's 250 million users. This is positive as CoStar is demonstrating that it can drive meaningful traffic growth to its platform. We believe the acceleration in investment over the past two years should drive organic growth on its Homes.com platform and expand the company's addressable market. We believe investors are currently attributing negative equity value to this. Over the next five years, we believe CoStar's residential investment could add at least $1 billion to annualized revenue at a significantly accretive margin. This would result in a 33% increase in today's $3 billion in revenue and an approximate 50% increase in EBITDA. Longer term, we believe this investment opportunity is several multiples of $1 billion of revenue. CoStar continues to hire new people for its commercial real estate sales business and should begin to see a rebound in net new bookings this year with continued strength in its retention rates, despite implementing price increases across its suite of products. It continues to have a strong balance sheet, with $2 billion of cash and just $1 billion of debt. We are not concerned with its residential investment and believe it should generate strong returns over time.

Property and casualty (P&C) insurance software vendor Guidewire declined 12.6% in the fourth quarter and detracted 62 bps from performance. However, the company continues to do quite well and after a multi-year transition period, the company's cloud transition is substantially complete and insurers are upgrading to the cloud at an accelerated rate. We believe that cloud will be the sole path forward, with annual recurring revenue ('ARR') benefiting from new customer wins and migrations of the existing customer base to the company's Insurance Suite Cloud. We also expect the company to shift R&D resources to product development from infrastructure investment, which should help drive cross-sales into its sticky installed base and potentially accelerate ARR over time. We are encouraged by Guidewire's subscription gross margin expansion, which improved by approximately 280 bps in its most

recently reported quarter. We believe Guidewire will be the critical software vendor for the global P&C insurance industry, capturing 30% to 50% of its $15 billion to $30 billion total addressable market and generating margins above 40%.

data.title: eBay Is Finally Worth Bidding For (Upgrade)
data.description:
I have a confession to make. I have always loved playing chess. But in recent years, I have become obsessed with chess collecting. This includes everything from sets to pens to stamps to trophies and so much more. The biggest focus of my collection, however, would be chess clocks. As of this writing, I own 116 of them, one of which dates back as early as 1890. And almost all of them have been purchased since late 2022. I call it speedrunning to bankruptcy. While some of these have been purchased from other collectors in the space, as well as other miscellaneous websites and auctions, I would venture to say that more than half of them have been acquired from eBay Inc. (EBAY). So naturally, when I saw shares of the online auction site jump around 3.1% on Feb. 19 after management announced financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year, and in response to the company informing investors that the business had acquired another online property, I felt as though it was a good time to revisit my previous thesis on the business.

My last article about eBay was published way back in July of 2020. In that article, I argued that the business deserved more of a "Hold" rating than anything else. Although I acknowledged that synergies associated with another acquisition that the business decided on could provide upside for investors, I believed that the stock was not cheap enough to justify a bullish outlook. In that time since then, shares have risen 68.7%, falling woefully short of the 108.8% increase that the S&P 500 enjoyed. However, the pure fundamental improvements that we have seen, especially as of late, justify optimism. Admittedly, the number of active buyers on the platform is not growing like I would prefer it to. But management has been successful in ramping up revenue and boosting certain profitability metrics. And with this latest acquisition in play, I would say that some upside is warranted. Enough, at least, to upgrade the stock to a soft "Buy."

The Market Is Bidding eBay Higher

Fundamentally speaking, I would say that eBay is doing just fine right now. Take data for the most recent quarter as an example. On Feb. 18, management announced financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Sales came in hefty at $2.97 billion. In addition to representing the big increase over the $2.58 billion the company generated a year earlier, the sales reported by the company exceeded analysts’ expectations by a whopping $90.6 million.

Year over year, the company saw a small increase in the number of active buyers from 134 million to 135 million. But the big driver behind the increase in revenue was a 9.9% jump in GMV (gross merchandise volume). Management defines this as the total value of all paid transactions between users on its platforms, including things like shipping fees and taxes. This metric shot up from $19.32 billion to $21.24 billion. The fact that the number of active buyers inched up by only 0.7% is proof that the surge was driven by a similar number of buyers using the platform a lot more than they did a year ago. Judging by how my bank account has looked recently, I think that diagnosis is correct.

Management continues to make innovations to get users to buy on the platform. But it's not just about buying. It's also about selling. For instance, during the quarter, the company expanded eBay Live, which is its interactive livestream shopping experience. It's now available to customers in Germany and Australia. During the quarter, the business delivered its largest-ever slate of eBay Live holiday shopping events to stoke real-time engagement around things like collectibles, sneakers, fashion products, and even refurbished technology offerings. It continues to invest in improving its listing experience by rolling out the next-generation beta of the technology on iOS and Android for both new and reactivated sellers in the United States. This technology seeks to simplify the listing process for users.

Speaking of AI, the company also launched a new AI-powered card-scanning experience that uses the company’s Smart Lens technology to help with things like historical pricing, aggregating and sharing PSA population data, and more. And it also saw success with the third season of King of Collectibles: The Goldin Touch, which debuted on Netflix and was one of the top ten shows in seven countries that it aired in. There are other examples as well, such as a series of collaborations with certain cultural figures. One example is comedian Pete Davidson. And the company continues to benefit from additional partnerships and the rollout of certain guarantee programs. Perhaps most exciting was the company's decision to launch AI Activate in the UK. This is a program developed in collaboration with ChatGPT parent OpenAI whereby up to 10,000 small businesses will have access to AI tools and training so that they can boost their own productivity and fuel growth.

All of these create additional activity on the company's platform and serve as a way to improve customer stickiness. Normally, you would expect top-line growth to translate to higher profits as well. But that's not exactly what transpired here. Earnings per share actually contracted from $1.40 to $1.14, pushing net profits down from $680 million to $525 million. But the good news is that even this reading exceeded analysts’ expectations to the tune of $0.15. On an adjusted basis, earnings per share rose from $1.25 to $1.41. This translated to a rise in adjusted net income from $607 million to $648 million. Operating cash flow did shrink from $677 million to $595 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a nice pop from $604 million to $934 million. Finally, EBITDA for the enterprise grew from $558 million to $716 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the business for the last three completed fiscal years now. The overall trend for most of these metrics has been higher. Of course, net profits and operating cash flows are both down. But all the other profitability metrics have risen. This is a testament to the overall quality of the company and the fact that it is growing, even if that growth rate is not stellar. What's more, management has provided some thoughts about the future. They have not detailed guidance for 2026 in its entirety. But in the table below, you can see what their expectations are for the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year compared to the same time in 2025. Across the board, the company expects expansion. So that certainly has some investors excited. Add on top of this that the company continues to return capital to shareholders, including $2.5 billion dedicated to share buybacks and another $531 million paid out in the form of dividends, and there is a lot to be excited about.

When it comes to valuing the company, I think that the process is pretty straightforward. In the chart below, you can see how the stock is valued using three different metrics. To be honest with you, these multiples would typically make the business a fair value candidate as opposed to an undervalued one. But I do provide some wiggle room when it comes to high-quality companies that are showing continuous expansion. So honestly, I would say that the stock is teetering between being fairly valued and undervalued. But in this case, because of the rapid improvement we have seen recently, I would argue that assigning it a slightly undervalued designation is appropriate.

The earnings reported by management are not what ultimately fueled me to write this article. In fact, the item that really stoked my interest this time around was news that management struck a deal to acquire a company by the name of Depop. This happens to be a consumer-to-consumer fashion marketplace that focuses on Millennials and Gen Z. Management has agreed to acquire this business for $1.2 billion. We unfortunately don't really know much in terms of the financial picture of the company. But we know enough to gauge whether this is an appealing decision or not. For instance, during 2025, Depop processed $1 billion worth of annual GMS (gross merchandise sales). Management was clear in the investor presentation that this is not comparable to its measure known as GMV. But it is the closest thing that we do have to a comparable. The platform had 7 million active buyers on it at the end of last year, with almost 90% of those under the age of 34. It also boasts in excess of 3 million active sellers.

At first glance, the purchase looks rather pricey. In the first chart below, you can see how much, on an enterprise value basis, eBay is paying for the GMS it is receiving from the acquisition. This makes the company far more expensive than eBay is at the moment. The picture is a little better when it comes to the amount being paid per active seller. This number is about $400 compared to the $317.46 that eBay is going for. So even though it does look as though the company is paying more than it might be wise to do, when you consider that these younger generations are still in their early days of earning and that they should see their earnings increase even more, it's not unreasonable to pay some sort of premium. This is especially true when you consider that, in 2025, the annual GMS processed by the business was up nearly 60% in the United States compared to what it was a year earlier. This rapid growth should play well into the $10 billion worth of GMV that eBay already processes on its platforms.

Historically, eBay has been very much a U.S.-centric business. In recent years, GMV generated by the company in the United States has grown much more rapidly than international GMV has. Considering the rapid growth that Depop has achieved here at home, being able to focus even more on the U.S. market by acquisition certainly makes sense. But until we know more about the actual financial picture of Depop, there is certainly some uncertainty associated with the transaction.

Takeaway

Based on all the data provided, I would argue that eBay is well positioned for future growth. The company is an attractive candidate. And even though the stock might not be as cheap as I would like it to be, I am encouraged by the innovations made by management. Long term, I expect further growth to occur. And when you add on top of this expansion through acquisition, I think that there are reasons to be optimistic enough to rate this a soft "Buy."

data.title: Pending Home Sales Unexpectedly Fall 0.8% In January
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The National Association of Realtors (NAR) pending home sales index unexpectedly fell in January, dropping -0.8% to 70.9, its lowest level in series history. This was significantly worse than the expected 1.4% growth and marks a -0.4% fall from one year ago.

“Improving affordability conditions have yet to induce more buying activity,” said NAR Chief Economist Dr. Lawrence Yun. “With mortgage rates nearing 6%, an additional 5.5 million households that could not qualify for a mortgage one year ago would qualify at today’s lower rates. Most newly qualifying households do not act immediately, but based on past experience, about 10% could enter the market—potentially adding roughly 550,000 new homebuyers this year compared with last year.”

“Unless housing supply increases, these additional potential buyers becoming active in the market could simply push up home prices. This will put increasing pressure on affordability, which is why it is critical to increase supply by building more homes. Fortunately, the House of Representatives recently passed the Housing for the 21st Century Act with strong bipartisan support, an important signal that addressing the nation’s housing shortage remains a shared priority. The legislation is a meaningful step toward expanding housing supply and removing barriers that make it harder for Americans to achieve homeownership,” Yun added.

Read more

Pending Home Sales Background

The pending home sales index (PHSI) was created by the National Association of Realtors to track home sales where the contract is signed, but the transaction has not yet closed. An index of 100 is equal to the level of contract activity in 2001. The PHSI is a leading economic indicator of future existing home sales.

The chart below gives us a snapshot of the index since 2001, the first year data was analyzed.

Over this time frame, the US population has grown by 20.9%. For a better look at the underlying trend, here is an overlay with the nominal index and the population-adjusted variant. The focus is pending home sales growth since 2001.

The above chart shows the percent off turn-of-the-century values. The index for the most recent month is currently 45% below its all-time high from August 2020. The population-adjusted index is 52% off its high from April 2005.

Pending Home Sales vs. Existing Home Sales

Pending home sales measures the change in the number of homes under contract to be sold but still awaiting the closing transaction. Whereas existing home sales measure the number of home sales by mortgage closings.

The NAR explained that "because a home goes under contract a month or two before it is sold, the Pending Home Sales Index generally leads Existing-Home Sales by a month or two." The pending home sales index is considered a more relevant and accurate measure of the real estate market.

Here is a growth overlay of the two series. The general correlation, as expected, is close. A close look at the numbers supports the NAR's assessment that their pending sales series is a leading index. Since 2001, pending home sales have fallen 30.1%, while existing home sales have fallen 23.3%.

The 30-Year Fixed Mortgage Rate

According to Freddie Mac, the average 30-year fixed-rate mortgage in January 2026 was 6.10%, the lowest level in over a year. Here is a look back at the 30-year fixed-rate mortgage weekly average, beginning in April 1971.

ETFs associated with residential real-estate include iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ).

data.title: The Great Astera Labs Reset
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Although the 30% reset in Astera Labs, Inc. (ALAB) since my last coverage, driven by near-term margin pressure and sequentially slowing growth rates, the investment thesis has not been broken. The bullish thesis still stands, as Astera is structurally embedded in the hyperscaler AI architecture and has Scorpio X as a high ASP scale-up anchor to drive content growth per rack. In addition, the AWS warrant is a long-term indicator of alignment and revenue visibility.

Q4 Performance: Strong But Growth Momentum Slowed

I see a company where sales are still growing at a rapid pace but at a pace that appears to be slowing. Sales have more than doubled for the year to date, free cash flow margins are strong, and the company ends the year with a net cash position and zero debt. The foundation remains strong. What’s changed isn’t the financial foundation. What’s changed is the pace. There has also been a step-down in the rate of operating margins. There has been a reduction in GAAP operating margins, which have declined from the mid-20% range to below 20%. There has also been a step-down in adjusted operating margins.

The company is not seeing weakness in demand. It is seeing an increase in the amount of reinvestment in scale-up fabrics, optical connectivity, and next-generation switching. The company has made investments of $93.8 million in research and development in the fourth quarter of the year. This is nearly 35% of the revenue. This is likely to go higher. The operating expenses are increasing more rapidly than the revenue in the short term. This is because the company is investing in scale-up fabrics, optics, and next-generation switching. The market is discounting the optics. However, the financials are very strong.

The Protocol Debate: Noise vs. Structure

I see a debate between UALink and Ethernet Scale-Up Networking. The assumption is that there is a winner-takes-all situation. This is not the case in the infrastructure business. There are winners and losers. The losers have a very painful ride. Why is PCIe better for low-latency memory-centric communications in the data center? UALink is optimized for the same.

What about scale-out networking? Ethernet is the obvious choice. What about the latency differences? UALink is in the hundreds of nanoseconds. Ethernet scale-out networking is in the microseconds. I view Astera's strategy as very pragmatic. Astera is participating in PCIe, CXL memory expansion, UALink, and increasingly NVLink Fusion. Astera is not betting on the protocol. Astera is betting on the architecture.

The more important question is not who succeeds. The more important question is that we recognize the emergence of cluster density. As ALAB increases the number of GPUs that communicate in the scale-up fabric, it will need more switching, retimer, signal conditioning, and memory connectivity. The increase in content per rack is an absolute need that is independent of architecture. That is where the future opportunity resides.

Scorpio X: The Architectural Lever

Scorpio X is not only a new product cycle; it is a step up in Astera's business model. Scorpio X is a scale-up switching solution, unlike Scorpio P, which is a scale-out switching solution. Scale-up switching has a higher ASP and dollar content in the accelerators. Astera has framed scale-up switching as a multibillion-dollar business opportunity in the latter half of the decade. If Scorpio P and X, in combination, are greater than or equal to 50% of the revenue the next year, Scorpio X will be the growth driver of the business.

Scorpio X will be the anchor socket, which will increase the attach rate and drive the sales of retimers, memory controllers, and other connectivity products. The key to the execution of Scorpio X is the qualification timing. The initial sales will be low, and the sales will increase in 2027. If the hyperscaler ramps line up with Scorpio X, the mix will shift to higher-margin fabric switching. If the qualification timing slips, the risk of revenue concentration will increase. Scorpio X will be the upside and the risk.
