The key to the execution of Scorpio X is the qualification timing. The initial sales will be low, and the sales will increase in 2027. If the hyperscaler ramps line up with Scorpio X, the mix will shift to higher-margin fabric switching.

If the qualification timing slips, the risk of revenue concentration will increase. Scorpio X will be the upside and the risk. The risk here is the timing mismatch. If the rate at which the revenues increase from Scorpio X and its adjacent scale-up products is not greater than the increase in R&D intensity, the pressure on the margins will continue.

The opportunity here is to benefit from the operating leverage that will be available once the scale-up products go into volume production, at which time the additional revenues will have high margins. Semiconductor products tend to have very sharp inflection points in their margins once the fixed investment phase of the investment curve stabilizes.

Valuation Reset After the Pullback

The valuation of the company after the pullback to the $129 price level has now reset to a much lower valuation but remains at a premium to the peer group average. The forward P/S valuation compression to 15.6x for 2026 and 12x for 2027 revenues is a sharp compression from the previous valuation levels that operated above 20x forward P/S. On the forward P/E valuation metric, the current valuation operates at 50x 2026 EPS and 35x 2027 EPS, which are high but not at the same valuation extremes as before the pullback.

It should be noted that the current revenue growth estimates are maintained at a growth rate close to 58% in 2026 and ~38% in 2027, reaching ~$2.35 billion in 2028 before normalizing. This warrants a premium valuation but not perfection pricing as before the pullback.

The current price trading at current levels means that valuation is not as extended as before but remains at a premium valuation based on the scale-up adoption and Scorpio-X ramp execution. To summarize, the pullback reduces risk but remains dependent on high growth and architectural expansion.

The Make-or-Break Variable

The most important factor which could either make or break the success of Astera in the coming years is the execution concentration on the Scorpio-X product. In the coming two years, the proportion of the revenues generated from the scale-up switching products will increase, and if the ramp rate of the revenues generated from the Scorpio-X product slows down because of the delay in qualification from hyperscalers, timing, and competition, the revenues will slow down very fast.

Since the stock is already at a premium, a slight slowdown in execution will cause the multiple to go down again. However, this risk is mitigated to a certain degree by the high level of customer engagement, participation in multiple protocols, and the high level of switching costs associated with AI clusters when the products are qualified. Nevertheless, the timing issue cannot be eliminated, and the sensitivity will increase the impact of the timing issue.

Takeaway

I see the pullback in Astera is simply a slowdown in sequential growth and reinvesting their margins. There are no underlying issues with them. They are extremely embedded in the hyperscaler roadmap, and Scorpio-X is ready to increase the content per rack. They are cash-rich and debt-free with a tight relationship with AWS. Volatility is rising, but so is their architectural relevance.

data.title: Block: The Bitcoin Facade Hiding A Double-Digit Core Growth Story
data.description:
Overview

Although this is my first formal evaluation of Block, Inc. (XYZ), I have a lot of familiarity with the company and its products. The one thing that struck me as I was reviewing some numbers was Block's (apparently) anemic growth and surprisingly low cash generation—just 0.5% YoY revenue growth and 5.2% free cash flow margins over the past two years.

Just a few years ago, Block—formerly Square—was an up-and-coming fintech, firing on all cylinders. But some things have happened since then.

Block was a beneficiary of pandemic-driven tailwinds that saw consumers switch to digital payments. The company’s foray into Bitcoin—a major contributor to growth during the 2021 crypto bull run—now drags its bottom line. Add in decelerating user spending, sector uncertainty, increased competition (e.g., Toast (TOST) and Fiserv’s (FISV) Clover), and Afterpay integration costs—and you can begin to understand why XYZ trades where it does today.

But that’s in the past.

The article that follows zeroes in on Block’s current valuation (and what it implies about future expectations) and examines how realistic it is (or not) for the company to turn things around.

Q3 Performance

Block’s revenue growth is being dragged down by Bitcoin’s (BTC-USD) performance.

You see, Block allows Bitcoin purchases by acquiring the asset and reselling it to users for a tiny premium. Q3 Bitcoin revenue—$1.97 billion—was down 19% YoY (impacting its Cash App performance).

If you peel away Bitcoin-related volatility, Block’s core services (Square & Cash App) are growing in the double-digits.

For Square, Block’s “upmarket” strategy is paying off. Its 12.2% GPV growth was thanks to “mid-market” sellers—who now represent nearly half of Square’s total volume. And larger sellers tend to be more “sticky,” which boosts the platform’s revenue durability.

Cash App continues to be popular. Subscription and service revenue grew 22.6%—thanks mostly to the Cash App Card and Cash App Borrow.

Q4/FY25 Preview

Ahead of the February 26 reveal and beyond the obvious (e.g., revenue/EPS beats, 2026 guidance, volume growth, etc.), I’m watching how Block’s “Rule of 40” (defined as "gross profit growth + adjusted operating income margin") aspirations are shaping up.

Last year, Block's board approved a $5 billion increase to its share repurchase program. The actual number of shares retired in Q4 could clue us in as to how confident the company is in future cash generation.

Financial Trends

I’m going to start out by displaying Block’s revenue growth and free cash flow margin trends over the last eight quarters—bearing in mind that its recent performance has been negatively impacted by Bitcoin.

Now, Block's free cash flow margin of 22.9% in model Q8 (Q3 2025) was due to many reasons. For starters, Block’s net income is surging—a 62.7% YoY increase in Q3 2025—reaching $461.5 million. So, some of this is due to real progress on profitability. Then, there are high non-cash add-backs like stock-based compensation, or SBC (+$309.4 million), and settlement timing/working capital changes (+$601.9 million).

So while it was a meaningful improvement, Block is clearly nowhere near 20% (normalized). It’s probably closer to ~13%—even with SBC. And, of course, on a quarterly basis, we can still expect to see ebb and flow.

Taking a closer look at some operational trends I monitor, what really pops out to me is Block's top-line improvements.

Gross margins improving from 35.4% to 43.8% within a span of two years is laudable. Meanwhile, easier-to-adjust figures—like R&D and capital intensity—remain quite low. While this is good for the bottom line, low investments here have their drawbacks (R&D/capex expenditures are needed for innovation). In any event, it is quite apparent to me that Block is likely to reach its "Rule of 40" goal.

Valuation Assumptions

As for model inputs, $27 billion for "Year 1 Revenue" is the consensus revenue estimate for 2026. "Revenue Growth" of 5% and "FCF Margin" of 7.5% are my estimates. These are based on historical performance and what I think can be reasonably achievable for Block. 5% Revenue Growth feels like a nice “middle ground” that accounts for Bitcoin-related volatility. Block's "Beta" varies by source. Seeking Alpha has it at ~1.5, which feels right and places the discount rate just under 10%.

Most everything else is either standard DCF methodology (i.e., "Terminal Growth" of 2%) or was derived from Block's most recent financial statements.

Next, I ran a Sensitivity Analysis on my DCF results. My focus is on revenue growth and free cash flow margin.

Block is 2.4x more sensitive to margins than it is to growth. So, it’s wise of them to focus on margins.

Next, I tried to think of growth and margin levers—actions Block can take to move these assumptions.

One thing that interests me is "AI-driven efficiency." The companies I’m most interested in this year are the ones that can implement AI to improve operations without having a “capex drag.”

Block appears well-positioned to take advantage of AI without having to spend billions in infrastructure.

Then again, so do its competitors—PayPal (PYPL) and Toast. But I think there’s room for multiple players.

So where does Block fit in for investors interested in this space?

Block can be viewed as the “Goldilocks” of fintech—faster than PayPal but significantly cheaper than the “pure-growth” Toast.

Moat

With AI-related existential threats impacting the software industry valuations, it would be ignorant of me not to address “moat.” Many are wondering why AI is moving valuations in software so much—even when material impact still seems years down the road. The answer is multifactorial.

Generative AI and “agentic workflows” are lowering "barriers to entry" across software—making disruption more achievable. This erodes “terminal value” and increases the cost of equity for software across the board.

This is why—even as some SaaS companies are "beating and raising"–multiples are compressing.

For Block, its data advantage (proprietary transaction history) and integrations (i.e., Square Financial Services) will help insulate it from AI risks. AI can write a few hundred lines of code, but it can't open and run a bank, too! Of course, seamless integration of AI into its own products is crucial, too. Square AI, for instance, is becoming an “automated manager” for SMBs—handling things like inventory refills and staffing schedules.

Block doesn’t just have nightmares about robots, though. It has to deal with vertical disruption from the likes of Toast (restaurants) and Shopify (SHOP) (e-commerce) and Big Tech entry from the likes of Apple (AAPL) (“Tap to Pay on iPhone”).

Fortunately, over the years, Block has transformed from merely a “credit card reader” to an operating system. So, it's not as easy for businesses to leave Square (switching costs are high).

Final Words

After stripping away the Bitcoin-related facade, Block's double-digit core revenue growth and improving profitability look particularly strong for a company its size/age. My discounted cash flow model suggests that Block is priced as if it has reached terminal growth—but we know this is not yet the case. For those interested in fintech—who can also stand some volatility—XYZ looks like a good choice (initiate “Buy”) at these prices.

Risks to my thesis include AI disruption, competition, credit risk (Afterpay), and regulatory scrutiny (i.e., potential changes to interchange fee regulations).

data.title: UTG: What A Real 'Dividend Growth' Machine Looks Like
data.description:
Readers familiar with my investing philosophy know that I am a big fan of using high cash income investments to "create my own growth" through reinvesting and compounding. This tends to cause me to invest mostly in corporate credit asset classes, like senior secured loans, high-yield bonds, business development companies ("BDCs"), as well as other high-yielding assets (debt and equity) that generate interest or dividend income in the high single digits or even low double digits.

This allows me to target the typical historical average "equity return" of 9%-10% per annum without having to be dependent on capital gains, like investors in traditional equity, like the S&P 500, whose cash yield only averages about 1.5%. With such a low cash yield, typical equity "growth" investors need to achieve a capital gain averaging about 8% every year to make up the difference between 1.5% and their 9%-10% target return.

For me and many of my readers, investing in loans, bonds, and other consistently high-paying fixed-income assets, whose reinvested and compounded cash flows eliminate most or all of the need for capital gains, allows us to sleep better at night through volatile market periods, avoiding the angst of equity investors earning puny yields while waiting for markets to recover. The icing on our cake is our realization that when prices drop, even on our own assets (which has been happening lately!), as well as the market in general, we can grow our income at a faster rate than ever, since we can reinvest and compound at bargain prices, which means higher reinvestment yields.

UTG, A Fund That Does It All

The Reaves Utility Income Fund (UTG) is a fund that lets us have it all: (1) a solid, steady cash flow that continues through thick and thin, all sorts of stock markets and economic cycles, plus (2) some serious income growth over the more than two decades since its inception in 2004.

To put some numbers on that:

  • UTG was started in 2004, 22 years ago this month;
  • Since that time it has increased its annual dividend 13 times, most recently last June.
  • Its total return has also been impressive:

The above chart is as of the end of 2025. When you add in the last month and a half, UTG has slightly beaten the S&P 500 in total return since its inception in 2004, with this current chart showing UTG in blue and SPY in red over that period (total return includes both dividends and capital gains/losses).

Wed 2004-02-25 to Fri 2026-02-13 OverallReturn
SPY × +791.68% +10.47%/yr
SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust Exchange-Traded Fund
UTG × +924.92%. +11.17%/yr
Reaves Utility IF Mutual Fund

Source: Total Real Returns.

Unlike the S&P 500 (SPY), however, UTG has generated its 11% average total return largely via its annual cash yield, which has ranged mostly between 6% and 7%, with the rest from capital gains in the 4%-5% per year range. As shown earlier, S&P's cash yield averages only about 1.5% per annum, so it is twice as dependent on capital gains to meet its historical total return target as UTG is to meet an equivalent total return level.

As its name suggests, UTG focuses on the utility and infrastructure sectors, describing its investment objective in similar language to many other closed-end funds:

The investment objective of the Fund is to provide a high level of after-tax total return consisting primarily of tax-advantaged dividend income and capital appreciation. It intends to invest at least 80% of its total assets in dividend-paying common and preferred stocks and debt instruments of companies within the utility industry. Up to 20% of the Fund may be invested in the securities of other industries.

When UTG says it seeks to generate "tax-advantaged dividend income," it isn't kidding. It consistently does, with between 95% and 100% of its dividends typically classified as "qualified" for income tax purposes. That means UTG is an ideal choice for accounts that are taxable, as well as for those that are tax-deferred, like IRAs.

UTG's portfolio, by industry, comprises:

  • Utilities 58.5%
  • Telecommunications 18.0%
  • Real Estate 7.9%
  • Energy, Oil & Gas 8.6%
  • Industrial, Capital Equipment 7.0%

The Reaves Asset Management team includes three managers, all of whom have been with the firm for over 20 years. Their stated investment philosophy is:

Reaves Asset Management believes that companies' characteristics drive their investment outcomes. Reaves favors companies which possess some, or all, of the following characteristics:

  • Operate in businesses with high barriers to entry

  • Face limited competition/higher regulatory scrutiny

  • Consistent, sustainable cash flow generation

  • Profitable in both up and down economic cycles

  • Dividends are viewed as an important component of capital allocation

This is very consistent with my own investment philosophy and would be difficult to disagree with. But more important, UTG has really executed on it and has the results to prove it, both recently and over the past 20+ years.

The reason UTG has been able to consistently increase its own dividend payout (13 times) while also continually boosting its NAV and market price over the past 22 years has been its ability to select investments (utilities and other companies) that, themselves, consistently increase their own dividends regularly.

The fund recently announced the dividend growth of its own portfolio holdings, and it was quite impressive:

  • As of 12/31/2025, UTG owned 64 common stocks, 51 of which paid a cash dividend in Q4 2025. In the past 12 months, 44 of these companies increased their dividend, four maintained the same payment, one company initiated a dividend, and two companies reduced their dividend. The overall weighted average dividend increase of UTG's investment portfolio was 6.2%.
  • At the end of 2024, UTG owned 55 stocks, and 44 of those increased their dividend that year, for an adjusted weighted average dividend increase of 7.2%. It says in a footnote that it was actually much higher, but because one of their investments, PG&E Corp., increased its dividend by 150%, they didn't include it in calculating the average. That suggests to me the fund, in addition to its other attributes, must be somewhat of a "real Boy Scout" in its reporting, which I like.
  • In 2023, UTG owned 56 stocks, of which 48 increased their dividends, for a weighted average increase overall of 7.9%. They "fess up" in a footnote that the percentage increase number includes a 100% increase from one firm, Constellation Energy. They say that if that were excluded from their calculation, the average increase would have "only" been 6%.

Bottom Line

Given its fine record, it's no surprise UTG has been on my radar as well as in my portfolio for many years. The market, recognizing its solid performance, has maintained its price near par (i.e., NAV) or at a small premium for a number of years. Currently, it has dropped to a discount just slightly below NAV, so this may be as attractive a time as ever to create or build positions. I look for long-term opportunities, not for trading vehicles, and believe UTG has proven itself to be a real "keeper."

data.title: SCHG's Upgrade Engine Is Losing Steam - Time To Temper Expectations
data.description:
The last time I wrote about the Schwab US Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG), I had benchmarked its expected performance against VUG and rated it a Hold. My arguments were that SCHG is slower to adapt and more thematically anchored to tech. That made it less positioned for a regime of valuation pressure I saw ahead. That part of SCHG's thesis has played out as expected. VUG has held up better in a period of consolidation since. And the difference is a few basis points - again that is the extent of arbitrage that should be expected given the broader growth alignment and a lot of commonality between the two ETFs. I do not expect the SCHG underperformance to grow beyond a couple of percentage points for context over a longer consolidation or turning regime.

This thesis is not on the comparative view but on SCHG's own outlook ahead as the volatile consolidation phase continues, in my view. In my earlier thesis too, I had formed the view around long-term growth prospects staying intact but broad tech-led multiple expansion slowing and returns becoming more selective rather than driven by widespread rerating. This thesis gauges the market situation through a raw data lens (earnings upgrades) and argues why SCHG's path ahead remains a Hold, with another round of quarterly results reported since my last thesis.

Earnings Upgrade Momentum

I have collated the 6-month and 3-month earnings revision trends from Seeking Alpha analyst estimates/revisions and checked whether the 6-month upgrade trend is materially higher than the most recent 3-month period (for FY1). The assumption is that it should support a hypothesis that the latest earnings cycle is weaker than the prior ones, i.e., upgrade momentum is decelerating. This analysis looks at the top 8 holdings in SCHG that together account for ~55% of the portfolio.

Across all the mega-cap stocks, the revisions we have seen in the past 3 months have been weaker than the levels we were seeing in the prior period. The only exception to that claim is Tesla, but revisions there are weak anyway. Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Apple Inc. (AAPL), and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) are still seeing some meaningful upgrades, but nowhere near the earlier levels.

Using FY2 as an outer year growth proxy, we see similar trends. This is not truly a great long-duration proxy, but it is the most practical given the amount of analyst coverage it gets (compared to, say, 2030). Here too, apart from Meta, all other mega caps have been seeing a slower pace of earnings upgrades than we were seeing in the 6-month period. Revisions are still healthy; they simply show cooling trends and dispersion within the mega-cap group, not a panic button, but neither a euphoric rally supportive signal.

Emphasis On Earnings Upgrade

Another angle I wanted to test using this data is whether earnings upgrades are effective in keeping the markets excited as much as they were in the peak of the 2025 rally. To do that, the table below compares revisions to Y1 and Y2 (an early proxy for long-duration outer years) in the past 3 months and share price returns in the same period.

Three stocks need discussion here—Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), Alphabet, and Broadcom Inc. (AVGO). These are the three stocks with the maximum earnings upgrades in either Y1 or Y2 (combined). Of these, both Google and Meta have seen relative strength in share prices. Google clearly has stronger revisions across both years. Meta also sees material earnings revisions in Y2 (although Y1 is weak). Meta also has not been trading near the highs, unlike the rest of the pack, so that helps share prices too. Broadcom has remained subdued despite healthy upgrades, and this can be attributed to a slowing upgrade momentum (discussed in the preceding section).

All in all, earnings upgrades do seem to be an important factor in determining share prices in the current regime, especially after a bull rally in 2025. And healthy upgrades alone are not enough; markets need a positive momentum in these upgrades to start rewarding the stocks. In today's environment, where upgrade acceleration is selective and becoming rarer, the odds of markets staying in a dispersion and digestion phase become higher (rather than broader rerating across the board).

Implications

The analysis has limitations. It looks at short-term tactical data (3-month, 6-month revision trends). There are other factors at play. Such as CapEx in the case of Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), dragging sentiment (although that can be traced eventually to slowing earnings revision for FY1 and FY2 as seen), or recent corrections in Meta (that drove its relative 3-month outperformance, rather than earnings revisions alone). Narratives are also very different across the universe of analysis.

Despite the limitations, a slowing earnings upgrade momentum cannot be denied from the data we have. Equally, positive upgrades (as well as upgrade momentum) seem to have some impact on share prices. And the conclusions, if not applicable at the stock level across the board, still broadly uphold my hypothesis that they do coincide with what is shaping up as a market where dispersion and consolidation (or a slower grind up) is more likely.

Importantly, this thesis does not aim to imply a collapse. Earnings revisions are still broadly positive. The thesis tries to highlight a tougher time ahead for SCHG, with more than half the portfolio under pressure and looking for a re-acceleration in earnings revisions. Within the rest of the portfolio, the software plays are anyway reeling under an investor apathy for long-duration assumptions in an environment where the risks of AI commoditizing several software products are being debated.

What I see ahead in this environment is earnings growing into valuations, rather than leading to meaningful forward revisions. The best-case scenario for share prices in that case is consolidation. Hence, SCHG remains a Hold. I will await another round of earnings and see whether they re-accelerate earnings upgrades before jumping back in. Long-term investors may accumulate through an expected phase of consolidation, but the risk-reward balance does not imply an urgent need for fresh investments at this stage.
